file_name: Hernanda et al. - 2025 - Incremental learning approaches for dermoscopic image classification in teledermatology.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used incremental learning with a ResNet-18 convolutional neural network (CNN) to classify skin diseases in dermoscopic images. The study trained the model on the HAM10000 dataset and acne images, achieving 90% accuracy after incremental learning, and utilized Grad-CAM for interpretability in a teledermatology context.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of an incremental learning model using ResNet-18 on the HAM10000 and acne datasets. It includes specific methodology, hyperparameter configurations, and quantitative results (e.g., accuracy improvements from 87% to 90%), which are characteristic of a primary research study rather than a review.

title: Incremental Learning Approaches for Dermoscopic Image Classification in Teledermatology
brief_summary: This study investigates the application of incremental learning to enhance skin disease classification in dermoscopic images for teledermatology. The researchers utilized a ResNet-18 convolutional neural network trained on the HAM10000 dataset combined with additional acne images, initially processing 1,052 samples across eight classes before incorporating 800 new data points. The evaluation demonstrated that the base model achieved 87% accuracy, which improved to 90% after the incremental learning phase. Significant improvements in precision, recall, and F1-scores were observed for challenging classes such as Basal Cell Carcinoma and Dermatofibroma. The study also implemented a Streamlit application to demonstrate real-time classification capabilities and used Grad-CAM for model interpretability. These findings suggest that incremental learning creates adaptable and accurate AI models suitable for evolving clinical environments.

all_task_types.target: Discrete skin disease diagnostic categories (Basal Cell Carcinoma, Actinic Keratoses, Melanocytic Nevus, Benign Keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Melanoma, Vascular Lesion, and Acne).
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly focuses on 'dermoscopic image classification' using a ResNet-18 CNN to predict discrete diagnostic labels for eight skin disease classes. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-scores, with confusion matrices showing class-wise correct classifications. The task involves assigning input images to one of the predefined discrete categories.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A public dataset comprising various skin lesions, used as the foundation for the base model. It contains seven classes of skin lesions.

use.training: Used for the initial base model training (636 samples across 7 classes).
use.validation: Used for validation during base model training (191 samples across 7 classes).
use.testing: Used for testing the base model (95 samples across 7 classes).
use.other: Simulated incremental learning by adding 100 new images per class (700 total) to this dataset structure.

datasets_used.name: Acne Images
datasets_used.description: A private dataset of acne images collected by a teledermatology system, added as an eighth class to simulate real-world clinical scenarios and incremental learning.

use.training: Used for the initial base model training (70 samples).
use.validation: Used for validation during base model training (20 samples).
use.testing: Used for testing the base model (10 samples).
use.other: Simulated incremental learning by adding 100 new images to this class.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance improvements were described qualitatively (e.g., 'significant improvements') and quantitatively via metric comparisons (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000 + Acne (Combined).experiment: Base Model vs Incremental Learning Model
HAM10000 + Acne (Combined).notes: Overall accuracy improved from 87% to 90% after incremental learning. Macro and Weighted averages for Precision, Recall, and F1-score all improved from 0.86/0.87 to 0.90.

HAM10000 + Acne (Combined) - Class-wise.experiment: Base Model vs Incremental Learning Model
HAM10000 + Acne (Combined) - Class-wise.notes: Significant improvements observed in bcc (F1: 0.75 to 0.98) and df (F1: 0.60 to 0.81). Acne maintained perfect classification. Melanoma and Vascular lesions showed slight decreases in some metrics but maintained high accuracy. bkl achieved perfect classification in the incremental model.

architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning used; model modified from original 1000-class output to 8-class output. Specific pretraining weights (e.g., ImageNet) not explicitly named, but transfer learning is confirmed.

architecture_details.layers: Four convolutional layers for image processing; residual connections included. All layers except the final fully connected (FC) layer were frozen during training.
architecture_details.heads: Modified fully connected (FC) layer reconfigured for 8-class classification.
architecture_details.interpretability_module: Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping (Grad-CAM) applied to the model's fourth layer of image processing.

training_details.optimizer: Optimizer with momentum 0.9 (specific optimizer algorithm like SGD/Adam not explicitly named, but momentum is specified)
training_details.training_schedule: Two-phase training: Base model training for 50 epochs, followed by incremental learning phase for 50 epochs (epochs 51-100). Checkpointing system used to store model state dictionary, optimizer state, last completed epoch, and training loss history.
training_details.data_preprocessing: Center crop, resizing to 224x224, normalization (mean 0, standard deviation 1 per RGB channel).

classification_or_output_head.modification: Reconfigured from original 1000-class output to suit 8-class classification needs.

preprocessing_techniques: Cropping (specifically center crop to isolate skin lesions/acne and exclude irrelevant features like eyes/nose/mouth for acne images)
Normalization (standardizing pixel values to mean 0 and standard deviation 1 per RGB channel)
conclusions: Incremental learning improved the overall classification accuracy of the ResNet-18 model from 87% to 90% on the test set.

The incremental learning approach yielded significant performance improvements for challenging skin disease classes, specifically Basal Cell Carcinoma (bcc) and Dermatofibroma (df), with bcc achieving a perfect recall of 1.00 and an F1-score of 0.98.

The model successfully incorporated a new class (acne) without compromising performance on existing classes, maintaining perfect classification for acne, Benign Keratosis (bkl), and Melanocytic Nevus (nv) in the incremental phase.
Some trade-offs were observed in Melanoma (mel) and Vascular lesions (vasc), which experienced slight decreases in certain metrics, indicating potential areas for further optimization.

The study demonstrates that incremental learning allows AI models in teledermatology to adapt to new data and evolving clinical knowledge without requiring complete retraining, thereby maintaining model relevance and performance.

The integration of Grad-CAM visualizations confirmed that the model focuses on clinically relevant features, supporting its interpretability and potential for human-AI collaboration in dermatological diagnostics.
important_remarks: The study relies on a very small total dataset (1,052 initial samples + 800 incremental samples), which limits the statistical power and generalizability of the results.

The incremental learning phase introduces a new class (Acne) that was not present in the base HAM10000 dataset, simulating a real-world scenario but potentially inflating perceived adaptability compared to standard incremental learning benchmarks.

No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the significance of performance improvements; results are described qualitatively as 'significant'.
The dataset is derived from HAM10000, which is known to have class imbalance and potential selection bias; the study does not report on external validation on independent, diverse clinical datasets.

Trade-offs were observed in Melanoma (mel) and Vascular lesions (vasc) classes, where some metrics decreased slightly after incremental learning, indicating potential catastrophic forgetting or interference issues for specific classes.
The model was trained on a small batch size (4) and limited epochs (50 per phase), which may affect convergence and robustness compared to larger-scale training regimes.
The acne data was collected from a specific teledermatology system, introducing potential domain bias specific to that collection method and population, rather than a diverse multi-center dataset.
The study demonstrates a proof-of-concept using a Streamlit application but does not provide evidence of clinical deployment, user study, or real-world diagnostic impact.
